growth_records.medium: Minimal medium (MM) + 2% (w/v) trehalose (no yeast extract in production phase)

up.method: substrate depletion over 29 h; residual trehalose measured (17.4 mM) from ~58 mM initial

growth_records.cite: Liu, Hualan et al. Functional genetics of human gut commensal Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron reveals metabolic requirements for growth across environments. Cell Reports (2021). DOI:10.1016/j.celrep.2021.108789. [PMC8121099]
growth_records.snippet: The cell-free 3-keto-trehalose-containing culture supernatant used for fitness and enzymatic assays was obtained after 29 h of growth and contained 17.4 mM of trehalose and 7.9 mM 3-keto-trehalose.
growth_records.notes: Incidental biotransformation measurement (not a dedicated growth-physiology study); no biomass reported so only VOLUMETRIC rates derivable, not per-gDW. Initial trehalose = 2% (w/v) = 20 g/L; using anhydrous MW 342.3 g/mol -> 20/342.3 = 58.4 mM (if dihydrate MW 378.3 -> 52.9 mM). Consumed = 58.4 - 17.4 = 41.0 mM over 29 h -> 41.0/29 = 1.41 mM/h. 3-keto-trehalose produced 7.9 mM / 29 h = 0.272 mM/h. Product/consumed yield = 7.9/41.0 = 0.19 mol/mol (remainder consumed for growth/oxidation). A. rhizogenes (alphaproteobacterium) periplasmically oxidizes trehalose to 3-keto-trehalose. Organism/condition is peripheral to the paper (which is a Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron VPI-5482 Tn-seq gene-fitness study; Bt itself reports only qualitative OD/fitness growth, no numeric mu or uptake/secretion rates, hence no Bt record).
